title: content::process_content corrupts HTML with naive string find/replace
description:
process_content() in content.rs uses result.find() and string slicing to replace hashtags, mentions, and URLs. This approach has multiple corruption vectors:
1. It replaces only the first occurrence of each URL/hashtag/mention, so the second identical URL in a post is left bare.
2. After auto-linking a URL, the result string grows, but subsequent find() calls may match inside the just-inserted <a href="..."> attribute text, producing nested/broken anchor tags.
3. The URL auto-linker collects matches from the original result, then replaces one at a time, but each replacement shifts byte positions so later replacements hit the wrong offset.
4. Hashtag regex captures from the original html but replacements target the already-URL-linked result string, causing mismatches.
All of these produce malformed HTML that Mastodon and other AP implementations may render incorrectly or reject. A proper fix requires an HTML-aware approach (e.g., walking DOM text nodes).
close_reason: process_content rewritten with position-aware right-to-left replacement, skipping existing <a> tags
